metadata.summary: CQRS separates reads and writes into distinct models; commands return void/ID, queries return data. Allows each side to evolve independently.

content.title: CQRS Pattern
content.summary: Command Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS) separates read and write operations into distinct models: commands change state and return void or an ID; queries return data and never modify state. The concrete rule: a handler class is either a CommandHandler (returns None or ID) or a QueryHandler (returns a read model) — never both. This split allows each side to evolve independently: write side uses the domain model with full invariant enforcement; read side uses flat projections optimized for query patterns (Redis, Elasticsearch, denormalized SQL views).

why.p: When a single model serves both reads and writes, optimizing one side degrades the other — a normalized write model forces expensive joins for reads, while a denormalized read model complicates write-side validation. CQRS allows each side to evolve independently: write side uses the domain model with full invariant enforcement; read side uses flat projections optimized for query patterns (Redis, Elasticsearch, denormalized SQL views).

list.item: High read/write ratio where each side has different optimization requirements
Complex domain with separate read models needed per use case
Systems requiring audit trails where events drive read model projections
Applications with eventual consistency requirements (microservices, event-driven)
Paired with event sourcing where projections rebuild from the event log
Simple CRUD apps — the two-model overhead exceeds benefit when reads and writes are symmetric
Teams unfamiliar with eventual consistency — stale reads cause correctness bugs if the team does not design for them
Systems that need immediate read-after-write consistency and cannot tolerate any lag
Small domains where a single repository and DTO cover all query needs without duplication

section.name: command-side
section.p: Commands change state and return void or an ID. Each command has exactly one handler.

rule.statement: Commands return void or an ID — never a read model or DTO. A command changes state; state reading belongs to the query side.
rule.rationale: Returning data from a command forces the command handler to also build a read projection, coupling the two sides and preventing independent optimization.

rule.statement: Name commands in imperative voice: PlaceOrder, CancelOrder, ShipOrder. Each command has exactly one handler.
rule.rationale: One-to-one mapping makes routing explicit and predictable. Fan-out on the command side is a design smell — use domain events for fan-out.

example.title: Command and Handler Base Classes
PlaceOrderCommand Handler
example.note: Returns None. Domain events published after save. Order ID pre-assigned by caller (idempotent retry safety).

section.name: query-side
section.p: Queries read from the read store and return DTOs. They never modify state.

rule.statement: Queries never modify state. A query handler reads from the read store and returns a DTO — no writes, no side effects, no view counters.
rule.rationale: Side effects in query handlers break the CQS invariant: callers assume reads are safe to retry; hidden writes violate that assumption.

rule.statement: Read models (DTOs) are flat and denormalized, optimized for the UI or API consumer. They are not domain entities and may duplicate data across multiple read models.
rule.rationale: Joins at query time on the write model defeat the performance optimization that justifies CQRS. Denormalized read models trade storage for query speed.

example.title: Query and Handler Base Classes
GetOrder Query Handler
example.note: Reads from Redis (the read store). No domain model touched. Returns a plain DTO.

section.name: projections
section.p: Projections subscribe to domain events and update the read store. Each event type has a dedicated handler method.

rule.statement: Projections subscribe to domain events and update the read store. Each event type has a dedicated handler method (_on_order_placed, _on_order_shipped). Projections must be idempotent.
rule.rationale: At-least-once event delivery means projections receive duplicates. Idempotent handlers (upsert, not insert) prevent read model corruption on replay.

example.title: OrderProjection
example.code:
class OrderProjection: def __init__(self, read_store: ReadStore): self._read_store = read_store
async def handle(self, event) -> None: handlers = { OrderPlaced: self._on_order_placed, OrderShipped: self._on_order_shipped, OrderCancelled: self._on_order_cancelled, } handler = handlers.get(type(event)) if handler: await handler(event)
async def _on_order_placed(self, event: OrderPlaced) -> None: await self._read_store.set( key=f"order:{event.order_id}", value={ "id": str(event.order_id), "customer_id": str(event.customer_id), "status": "placed", "total": float(event.total), "placed_at": event.occurred_at.isoformat(), }, ) await self._read_store.add_to_index( index="orders_by_customer", key=str(event.customer_id), value=str(event.order_id), score=event.occurred_at.timestamp(), )
async def _on_order_shipped(self, event: OrderShipped) -> None: await self._read_store.update( key=f"order:{event.order_id}", updates={"status": "shipped", "tracking_number": event.tracking_number}, )

example.title: Redis Read Store
example.code:
class RedisReadStore: def __init__(self, redis_client): self._redis = redis_client
async def get(self, key: str) -> Optional[dict]: data = await self._redis.get(key) return json.loads(data) if data else None
async def set(self, key: str, value: dict) -> None: await self._redis.set(key, json.dumps(value))
async def update(self, key: str, updates: dict) -> None: data = await self.get(key) if data: data.update(updates) await self.set(key, data)
async def add_to_index(self, index: str, key: str, value: str, score: float) -> None: await self._redis.zadd(f"{index}:{key}", {value: score})

section.name: mediator
section.p: The mediator dispatches commands to handlers and queries to handlers by type.

example.title: Mediator

rule.statement: Commands must never return a DTO or full entity. If the client needs the resulting state, it must issue a separate query after the command completes.
rule.rationale: Commands returning data forces the handler to also build a read projection inline, coupling the two sides and preventing independent scaling of read and write sides.

rule.statement: Queries must never have side effects. No incrementing view counts, updating last-seen timestamps, or state modifications inside query handlers.
rule.rationale: Callers assume reads are idempotent and safe to retry. Hidden writes violate this assumption, cause double-counting, and break eventual consistency reasoning.

antipattern.description: Command handler returns a DTO or full entity after saving, forcing the handler to also build a read projection inline.
antipattern.reason: Couples write and read optimization. Breaks the single-responsibility of commands. Prevents independent scaling of read and write sides.

example.title: Commands Returning Data
example.code: class CreateOrderHandler: async def handle(self, command) -> OrderDto: # Wrong return type order = Order.create(...) await self._repo.save(order) return OrderDto.from_entity(order) # Build read model in command handler

example.title: Command Returning ID
example.code: class CreateOrderHandler: async def handle(self, command: PlaceOrderCommand) -> UUID: order = Order.create(id=command.order_id, ...) await self._repo.save(order) return order.id # Or return None; caller uses a separate query to fetch state

antipattern.description: Query handler modifies state (increments view count, updates last-seen timestamp) as part of returning data.
antipattern.reason: Callers assume reads are idempotent and safe to retry. Hidden writes violate this assumption, cause double-counting, and break eventual consistency reasoning.

example.title: Queries With Side Effects
example.code: class GetOrderHandler: async def handle(self, query): order = await self._read_store.get(query.order_id) order["view_count"] += 1 # Side effect in a query! await self._read_store.set(order) return order

example.title: Pure Query

section.name: limitations-and-gotchas
section.p: CQRS introduces complexity that must be actively managed:

Eventual consistency UX leaks: Users place an order, navigate to "My Orders", and don't see it. Without a read-your-writes strategy (sticky session on write store, optimistic UI, command-side return of projected DTO) you get bug reports indistinguishable from data loss.

Projection drift: The read store gets out of sync with events because of a deploy-without-rebuild, a swallowed exception in the projector, or a schema change on the DTO. There must be a deterministic "rebuild from event log" command, otherwise you ship corrupt reads.

Cross-aggregate queries: When a query needs data from 3 aggregates and there's no read model for that combo, teams either (a) build a 4th projection, (b) JOIN in the API layer (slow), or (c) call multiple services (chatty). All three are visible regressions vs. a single SQL query in CRUD.
Command idempotency: Replays, retries, and at-least-once buses cause double-charges if command_id is not the dedup key. Production implementations need an idempotency_key table or saga state.

Bus-as-magic: The mediator pattern hides handler resolution; debugging a "no handler found" or a slow handler chain across DI containers is painful. Static handler registries beat runtime discovery for agent-readability.
Versioned events: Once you have projections you can't change event payloads freely; you need event versioning + upcasting. Most teams discover this the first time they want to add a field.
Saga creep: Multi-aggregate workflows (Place → Charge → Reserve Stock → Ship) are not CQRS — they're sagas/process managers. Teams shoehorn sagas into command handlers and the model collapses.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive CQRS scaffolding as a four-stage pipeline: (1) a design agent extracts the aggregate + command/query inventory from the spec; (2) a code-gen agent emits Command, CommandHandler, Query, QueryHandler, and Projection skeletons aligned to the repo's existing module structure; (3) a test agent generates property-based tests for command idempotency and projection eventual-consistency; (4) a review agent runs the anti-pattern checklist (commands returning data, queries with side effects, projections with business logic). Persist the inventory in .aidocs/product_docs/cqrs-inventory.md so subsequent feature work appends to it instead of forking a new model. Use faion-sdd-executor-agent to convert each new command/query into an SDD task with handler + test + projection update as one task unit.

Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent — turns each new command/query into a TASK with handler + test + projection update as a single unit; opus model fits well because handler/projection trade-offs are non-trivial. password-scrubber-agent — run before committing event payloads / fixtures; commands often carry PII that must not leak into test snapshots. A purpose-built cqrs-review-agent (worth adding): single-pass linter that greps handler files for canonical anti-patterns (command return type ≠ None|UUID, query handler with await *.save(), projection importing domain services, event without occurred_at). /faion skill — sequential execution mode is correct for CQRS feature slices: command before query before projection before integration test; out-of-order task execution corrupts the read model.

Prompt pattern — inventory pass: You are a CQRS architect. Given the feature spec in spec, produce a table of Commands and Queries. Columns: name, intent, returns (None/UUID for commands; DTO shape for queries), aggregate touched, events emitted (commands only), read store index used (queries only). Reject any command that returns a DTO. Reject any query that emits events. Output as markdown tables.

Anti-pattern review pass: You are reviewing a PR adding CQRS handlers. Flag any of: (1) command handler with return type other than None or UUID, (2) query handler that calls *.save / *.update / publish_event, (3) projection that imports from domain.*.services, (4) event class without occurred_at and aggregate_id, (5) handler registered both as command AND query handler. Cite file:line. Do not propose fixes — only flag.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Handler = one transaction: A command handler opens one unit of work, mutates one aggregate, emits its events, commits. If you need two aggregates, write a saga, not a fatter handler.
Stable command IDs from the client: Client generates command_id (UUIDv7) and the handler dedups on it. Solves at-least-once retries cleanly; production code must include this.

Projections are passive and rebuildable: No business rules, no service calls — only "read event → upsert read row". Keep a replay_projection(name, from_position=0) admin command; if you can't rebuild, you don't have CQRS, you have two databases that lie about each other.

Read-your-writes via DTO return: Have the API layer (not the handler) optionally project the just-committed aggregate to a DTO synchronously, return it, while the async projector still runs. Avoids the eventual-consistency UX trap without breaking the rule that the handler returns None|UUID.
One read model per query, not per aggregate: "OrderListByCustomer" and "OrderListByWarehouse" are two projections; do not try to share. Storage is cheap, JOINs at read time are not.
Event payloads are public API: Once a projector or external consumer is on an event, you can't rename a field. Use additive evolution + upcasters; never edit a published event class.
Mediator should be a dict, not a framework: Static {CommandType: Handler} registration in one composition root beats DI auto-discovery for agent-readability and grep-ability.
Test the projection contract, not the projector code: Write event log → expected read store rows; this catches schema drift across deploys far better than unit tests on the projection class.
Cap query DTO size: Pagination + projection narrowing — never let a query handler return a list >page_size; agents will write limit=10000 and you'll OOM the read store.

template.purpose: Base Query + QueryHandler + OrderDto read model skeleton
Mediator for dispatching commands and queries by type
